title: PVP005 500 COMBAT ANIMATION AUDIT
method: vision_analyze on 8-frame walk / 16-frame combat contact sheets
finding: All frames identical — characters completely frozen

defects.defect: All 16 frames show identical character poses
Tick 20 through tick 35 produce zero visible change
16 consecutive ticks of frozen combat interaction
Combat animation is completely absent during combat phase
The combat interaction loop has no visual progression
What should be a dynamic exchange is a static tableau
Two characters frozen in eternal contact
Strike, block, grab — none animate
The entire combat action set is unrepresented in animation
All combat states collapse to one frozen pose
The frozen pose persists across truth frame boundaries
Truth frames advance (24→33→39) while poses don't
Combat truth state changes have zero visual effect
PlanPhase resolves combat actions but doesn't drive animation
Combat resolution occurs in code, invisible to player
The game calculates who wins but can't show it
Combat is a mathematical exercise with graphical stasis
The most important gameplay moments have no animation
Contact YES displayed continuously across all 16 frames
Continuous contact with zero reaction is physically impossible
Real combat has impact, recoil, separation — this has none
The frozen contact state is physically implausible
Characters appear to be gently touching for 16 frames
Gentle touch is not combat
The contact state should transition — it doesn't
Contact should produce reaction — it doesn't
Reaction should produce recovery — it doesn't
Recovery should return to neutral — it doesn't
The entire contact→reaction→recovery chain is missing
Only the start of the chain exists: contact=true
Contact=true is a boolean, not an animation
A boolean cannot convey the quality of contact
Light touch and heavy strike both produce CONTACT YES
The animation cannot distinguish tap from smash
All contact is visually identical
Contact quality is lost in the boolean abstraction
The boolean interface to contact prevents nuanced animation
Contact should have magnitude, direction, location — all absent
Without contact parameters, animation has nothing to work with
The animation system receives "contact occurred" with no details
Garbage in, garbage out: no contact details → no reaction animation
The truth→presentation boundary transmits too little information
Truth knows contact details; presentation receives a boolean
Information loss at the truth→presentation boundary
The boundary is a one-bit channel for the richest game event
One bit cannot drive 500 animation quality points
The animation system is starved of input data
Even if animation playback worked, it would have nothing to show
Contact reaction requires contact parameters
Contact parameters are not exposed to the animation system
The animation system's input interface is too narrow
Widen the truth→presentation boundary
Expose contact point, normal, force, body part, weapon type
With rich input, animation can produce rich output
Currently: rich input (truth) → one-bit channel → starved animation
The pipeline starves its most important consumer
Animation is the last mile — and it gets the least data
Fix the data flow before fixing the animation
Data starvation is a systemic defect
The truth system knows everything; the animation system knows nothing
Combat interaction is data-rich in simulation, data-poor in presentation
The simulation simulates; the presentation doesn't present
Presentation is a thin veneer over rich simulation
The veneer is cracked — it shows nothing
Combat interaction animation fails at every level
Input: insufficient data
Processing: no playback integration
Output: frozen static pose
The failure is complete and systemic
Three independent failure modes converge on one symptom
Fixing one won't fix the others
All three must be addressed
Rich contact data → animation playback → visible reaction
The chain has three broken links
Each link is a separate engineering task
Link 1: expose contact parameters from truth to presentation
Link 2: wire animation playback into combat state rendering
Link 3: create reaction animation clips (or generate them)
All three links are missing
The combat animation system exists only in aspiration
Code for truth exists; code for presentation doesn't
The ratio of truth code to presentation code is infinite
Truth code: thousands of lines. Presentation code: zero lines
The project has built a simulation with no visualization
Combat is 100% simulated, 0% animated
This is a simulation, not a game
Games show the simulation; this one doesn't
The defining characteristic of a game is missing
Players cannot see what's happening
Players must read HUD text to understand combat
Combat is a text adventure with 3D graphics
The 3D graphics are vestigial
Remove the 3D viewport and the game is equally playable
The viewport adds zero information beyond the HUD
The HUD is the game; the viewport is decoration
Decoration that doesn't decorate is wasted rendering
Every frame rendered is a frame that could show animation
Instead, every frame shows the same frozen pose
16 frames of wasted rendering opportunity
Combat interaction animation: completely, totally, absolutely absent
Strike slash (22F) is listed but never animated
Strike thrust (18F) is listed but never animated
No wind-up phase visible for any strike
No weapon chambering (pulling back before strike)
No weapon acceleration (swing arc)
No weapon impact frame (contact moment)
No weapon follow-through (continuation past target)
No weapon recovery (return to guard)
The entire strike lifecycle is absent
Strike is a 4-phase action; zero phases are animated
Phase 1 (anticipation): missing
Phase 2 (execution): missing
Phase 3 (impact): missing
Phase 4 (recovery): missing
Strike timing (22F/18F) exists only as a number
22 frames of slash exist as a duration, not as animation
The slash duration implies 22 unique poses — all missing
22 frames of strike animation that don't exist
The frame data table is a wishlist, not an asset manifest
Every frame count in the HUD corresponds to zero animation frames
Strike slash: promised 22F, delivered 0F
Strike thrust: promised 18F, delivered 0F
The HUD promises what the animation can't deliver
False advertising in the game's own interface
Player sees "SLASH 22F" and expects a slash animation
Player gets a frozen mannequin
Expectation gap: maximum
The HUD creates expectations the animation violates
Silent promise, loud betrayal
Player trust erodes with every missing animation
First strike: "maybe it's loading"
Second strike: "is this a bug?"
Third strike: "this game has no animation"
Player conclusion is correct: the game has no strike animation
Strike is the most fundamental combat action

If strike doesn't animate, the game doesn't animate
Strike animation is the canary for combat animation

No weapon visible during strike attempt
Weapon mesh exists but is not rendered in combat pose

Strike animation without weapon is just arm waving
Arm waving is not strike animation
Even if arms moved, without weapon it wouldn't read as strike

Weapon + animation = strike; neither exists
Strike readability: 0%
Blind action-read test: 0% success rate
Canon requires ≥80% blind action-read success
Current state: 0% (80 percentage points below requirement)
Strike animation is the largest gap to canon compliance
Closing the gap requires weapon rendering + 22-frame animation clip
Neither exists
Weapon rendering: mesh exists, not positioned correctly
Animation clip: doesn't exist
Two missing pieces, one strike action
Building strike animation requires both pieces
Weapon positioning is the easier piece
Animation clip authoring is the harder piece
MotionBricks could generate strike clip but output is invalid
Existing hero_strike.413.f32 could be retargeted but isn't wired
The strike clip exists in G1 format, not in engine format
G1→C0 retargeting pipeline exists but isn't integrated
Multiple pieces exist; none are assembled
The strike animation assembly is blocked on integration
Integration of existing pieces is the path to strike animation
No new assets needed — just wire existing ones
Hero_strike clip: exists, valid, unused
Retargeting code: exists, tested, unused in game_loop
Weapon mesh: exists, loaded, not positioned for strike
All strike animation ingredients exist
The kitchen has all ingredients; no one is cooking
Assemble strike animation from existing components
Strike animation: possible with current assets, blocked on integration
Block (12F) is listed but never animated
No guard raise animation
No weapon repositioning for defense
No arm bracing for impact absorption
No body compression on block impact
No deflection of incoming strike
No recovery from block back to guard
Block lifecycle: 4 phases, zero animated
Block timing is 12F — faster than strike (22F)
Faster action should show quicker animation — shows nothing
Block animation should be snappy and reactive — it's absent
Block is a reaction to opponent's action
Reaction animation requires opponent action to exist first
Without strike animation, block animation has nothing to react to
Block animation is dependent on strike animation
Dependency chain: strike → block, both broken
Fixing block requires fixing strike first
Block is the second-order failure
First-order failure (strike) cascades to second-order (block)
The combat system has dependent animation failures
Dependency graph: all nodes broken
No animation node in the combat graph is functional
The entire combat animation graph evaluates to zero
Independent of specific action, all actions are equally broken
Strike, block, grab, dodge, feint, cancel — all identical failure
The failure mode is uniform across all actions
Uniform failure suggests a common root cause
Root cause: no action→animation mapping exists
The mapping from Intent to animation clip is empty
An empty mapping produces no animation for any action
Populate the mapping to fix all actions simultaneously
Block: map Intent::Block → block animation clip
Block clip doesn't exist — needs to be created
Block animation can be procedurally generated: cross arms, brace
Procedural block is better than no block
Ship procedural block before authored block
Procedural animation is an acceptable prototype
Prototype block animation: raise both arms in front of body
Even a simple arm raise would improve readability
Current block readability: indistinguishable from idle
Simple arm cross would be a 100× improvement
The bar for block animation is extremely low
Any movement is better than no movement
Block animation MVP: one pose, held for 12 frames
Single-pose block would pass the "is something happening?" test
Currently fails "is something happening?" — nothing happens
Block MVP would answer "yes, the character is blocking"
"Is blocking" is infinitely more informative than "is frozen"
Ship the simplest possible block animation immediately
A one-pose block is a 5-minute code change
5 minutes to go from 0% readable to 50% readable
ROI of block MVP is enormous
Block animation: easiest win in the entire project
Block MVP would be the first combat animation to work
Be the first animation to break the freeze
Grab (20F) is listed but never animated
No reach phase — arm doesn't extend toward opponent
No grip phase — hand doesn't close on opponent
No control phase — opponent isn't restrained
No release phase — grip doesn't open
Grab lifecycle: 4 phases, zero animated
Grab requires two-character interaction
Two-character animation is more complex than one-character
Both characters must animate for grab to read correctly
Attacker must reach; defender must react
Neither character animates — grab is doubly broken
Grab animation requires synchronized paired animation
Synchronized animation is the hardest type
Grab is the hardest action with the least animation support
CLINCH state exists in HUD but has no animation
CLINCH - displayed, meaning clinch is inactive
Clinch should activate at close range — never does
Clinch entry animation: closing distance, establishing grips
Clinch control animation: pummeling, position changes
Clinch exit animation: breaking away, creating distance

Complexity gradient is inverted

Complex actions (grab, clinch) should animate later

Prioritize simple actions for animation development
Strike > Block > Dodge > Grab > Clinch (difficulty order)
Start at the beginning of the difficulty chain
Grab animation is blocked on strike animation
Grab requires the opponent to be hittable first
If opponent doesn't react to strikes, won't react to grabs
Grab is the third-order failure
First: strikes don't animate

The entire combat interaction tree is barren
No fruit on any branch
Grab + clinch represent the deepest combat mechanics

7 actions unlocked by one feature

Required state: root is a function of animation
Root position should be driven by animation clip
Walking.anim contains root motion data — unused
The animation clip has root displacement; the engine ignores it
Root motion data exists in the clip, discarded by the engine
Engine extracts pose, discards root
Root extraction is a one-line code change
Extract root translation from animation clip
Apply root translation to character world position
With root motion, walking.anim would actually move the character

Currently: character walks in place (invisible)
With root motion: character walks across floor (visible)
